skill.name: safe-edit
skill.description: Adds a safety workflow for code edits by backing up files, saving diffs, tracking tasks, and checking file size before implementation.
skill.summary: Create reversible code edits with backups, diffs, task tracking, and modularity checks.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, weak-crypto, and entropy indicators. Review found the command and filesystem indicators are mostly documented workflow examples, but the skill intentionally asks agents to run shell commands, copy files, write backups, create diffs, and perform rollback or cleanup operations. No evidence found of malware, credential theft, network exfiltration, or prompt-injection text that overrides the audit process.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Write and Backup Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to create .backups directories, copy source files, write diff files under /tmp/diffs, and preserve rollback material. This is legitimate for a safe-edit workflow, but it grants broad filesystem effects and may duplicate sensitive file contents into backup or patch files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-copy and diff-writing workflow is explicit and repeated across the skill documentation. It is not malicious by itself, but it can expose sensitive content through backups and temporary patch files.

medium_findings.title: Shell Commands With Destructive or Broad Effects
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes shell operations such as patch reversal, git restore, git reset --hard, find with xargs, and deletion of older backups. These commands are presented as examples, but incorrect use can discard work or operate on more files than intended.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are directly visible and include operations with real data-loss potential. The context is rollback and maintenance, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Overbroad Automatic Activation Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to activate it for all code changes and any implementation request. This broad scope can affect tasks where the user did not request backups, diffs, shell commands, or extra generated files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The broad activation language is explicit in the skill text. It does not contain an audit bypass or system override phrase, but it can cause unexpectedly broad behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography locations point to documentation text and metadata, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic routines being executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited areas shows descriptions, paths, agent names, and command examples rather than cryptographic implementation. The static pattern appears to match text fragments, not executable crypto code.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-entropy warnings are on Markdown documentation files that contain Korean text, symbols, paths, and command examples. No evidence found of binary payloads, encoded scripts, or encrypted content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are readable Markdown guides, and the first lines show ordinary documentation content. The entropy heuristic is plausibly triggered by mixed-language text and formatting symbols.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Shell Commands in Workflow Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents mkdir, cp, git diff, patch, git restore, git reset, find, xargs, wc, sort, and head commands. These are expected for the workflow, but marketplace users should understand that the skill can request local command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell command blocks are explicit and central to the skill. The danger depends on user approval and execution context rather than hidden code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Temporary and Hidden Directory Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill stores backups in a hidden .backups directory and stores diffs in /tmp/diffs. These locations can retain sensitive source content outside normal version-control review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden and temporary storage paths are documented in multiple files. The concern is data retention and disclosure, not malicious exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Code changes can be hard to review or undo when backups and diffs are skipped. This skill adds a repeatable workflow for backups, patch files, task tracking, and rollback guidance.
actual_capabilities: Guides agents to create timestamped backups before modifying files.
Guides agents to save pre-change and post-change diffs for review.
Encourages task tracking for multi-step implementation work.
Checks file length and recommends modularization when files grow too large.
Documents rollback options using backups, reverse patches, or Git commands.
Suggests specialized agents for UI, API, database, and analysis tasks.
limitations: It is an instruction workflow, not a standalone backup application.
Shell commands still require correct paths and careful execution.
Backups and diffs may contain sensitive source content.
The 200-line rule is a guideline and may not fit every codebase.

use_cases.title: Safer Feature Changes
use_cases.description: Add a feature while preserving file backups, diffs, task status, and rollback notes.

use_cases.title: Reviewable Refactoring
use_cases.description: Break large files into smaller modules and keep a patch trail for review.

use_cases.title: Rollback-Ready Bug Fixes
use_cases.description: Fix production issues with explicit restore options if the change is not accepted.

prompt_templates.title: Back Up One File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use safe-edit for this small change. Back up the affected file, make the edit, save the diff, and report the rollback command.

prompt_templates.title: Implement a Feature Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use safe-edit to implement this feature. Create a task list, back up affected files, save diffs, verify the build, and summarize the changes.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Large File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use safe-edit to refactor this file. Check the line count, propose smaller modules, back up files, apply the refactor, and save a reviewable patch.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Multi-Agent Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use safe-edit for a multi-file implementation. Analyze the codebase, choose relevant specialist agents, preserve rollback material, run verification, and explain residual risk.

output_examples.input: Add a loading state to the dashboard component.
output_examples.output: The agent reports the backed-up dashboard file, the saved patch location, the implemented loading state, verification results, and the restore command.

output_examples.input: Refactor a large editor container into smaller modules.
output_examples.output: The agent presents a modularization plan, lists the new smaller files, summarizes the saved diff, and records how to reverse the change.

output_examples.input: Fix a bug but keep a rollback path.
output_examples.output: The agent identifies affected files, creates backups, applies the fix, runs available checks, and gives concise rollback instructions.

best_practices: Review backup and diff paths before running commands on sensitive repositories.
Keep rollback instructions with each completed change summary.
Run project-specific tests after applying safe-edit changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run destructive rollback commands without checking the current Git state.
Do not store secrets in files that will be copied into backups or diffs.
Do not treat the 200-line limit as a substitute for architecture review.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow instructions that may ask the agent to run local filesystem and Git commands.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses legitimate shell and filesystem workflows, but those workflows can copy sensitive files or discard local changes if misused.

faq.question: Where are backups stored?
faq.answer: The documentation uses .backups directories inside the project and /tmp/diffs for patch files.

faq.question: Can it help with large files?
faq.answer: Yes. It asks the agent to check file size and plan modularization when files exceed the documented threshold.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Should teams publish this without warnings?
faq.answer: No. Marketplace users should see a warning about filesystem writes, temporary diffs, and rollback commands.
